skill.name: recipe-manager
skill.description: Guides agents through adding, editing, converting, and validating CookMode V2 recipe entries in recipes.js.
skill.summary: Manage structured CookMode V2 recipe data with schema guidance and validation checks.
skill.author: AdamFehse

security_audit.summary: AI review dismissed the static external command, weak cryptography, browser credential, and reconnaissance alerts as false positives from Markdown and JavaScript examples. One real concern remains: the skill directs agents to an author-specific absolute path, which can cause unintended filesystem edits outside a user project. No evidence found of malicious intent, prompt injection, credential access, network exfiltration, or executable shell commands.

medium_findings.title: Hardcoded Absolute Filesystem Path
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents that recipes.js is located at an author-specific absolute path. This is not malicious, but it may lead agents to read or modify files outside the active workspace when used on another system.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains a concrete absolute path to recipes.js. The risk is contextual because the skill is designed to edit recipe data, but the path is not portable or workspace-scoped.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported Ruby or shell backtick execution across Markdown prose and fenced JavaScript examples. The reviewed content contains documentation, inline code formatting, object examples, and JavaScript template literals, not shell command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are in SKILL.md documentation and JavaScript examples. No shell command, Ruby code, or executable backtick invocation is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Matches Text
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported weak cryptographic algorithms, but the cited lines are recipe schema text, categories, ingredient parsing examples, and recipe names. No cryptographic API, hash function, cipher, or password storage logic is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not call or describe cryptographic operations. The matches appear to come from short substrings in ordinary documentation or example data.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Browser Credential File Alert
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged a browser credential file pattern, but the cited line is part of a chocolate chip cookie recipe example. No browser profile path, credential database, cookie store, or secret extraction logic appears at that location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 252 contains a recipe name field in a JavaScript object example. The static match is unrelated to credential files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Pattern Matches Documentation
low_findings.description: The scanner reported reconnaissance patterns, but the cited lines are a validation checklist item for image URLs and component documentation. No host enumeration, process listing, environment probing, or system discovery instruction is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text documents recipe images and app component names. There is no semantic evidence of reconnaissance behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Workspace-Unsafe File Target
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill names an absolute local path as the recipe file location. Marketplace users should adapt this to a workspace-relative path before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path is explicit and absolute. The risk depends on how an agent resolves file edits during use.

content.value_statement: Recipe files can become inconsistent when entries use different structures. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code clear rules for editing CookMode V2 recipe data.
actual_capabilities: Defines the required recipe object fields for CookMode V2.
Explains ingredient objects with amount, unit, ingredient, and prep fields.
Guides conversion from string ingredients to structured ingredient objects.
Provides validation checks for categories, slugs, components, and instructions.
Shows examples for notes, images, scaling, and display formatting.
Supplies a conversion prompt for transforming pasted recipes.
limitations: Assumes the CookMode V2 recipe schema and category list.
Does not include automated tests or validation scripts.
Contains an author-specific absolute path that should be changed before use.
Does not fetch recipe data or images from external services.

use_cases.title: Add New Recipes
use_cases.description: Create a recipe entry with structured ingredients, grouped components, clear instructions, and optional notes.
use_cases.target_user: Home cooks maintaining a CookMode V2 recipe file

use_cases.title: Standardize Existing Data
use_cases.description: Convert older ingredient strings into objects with amount, unit, ingredient, and preparation fields.

use_cases.title: Review Recipe Changes
use_cases.description: Check slugs, categories, syntax, notes, image fields, and component structure before committing recipe edits.

prompt_templates.title: Add a Simple Recipe
prompt_templates.prompt: Add this recipe to recipes.js using the CookMode V2 schema: [paste recipe name, ingredients, steps, and notes].

prompt_templates.title: Convert Ingredient Lines
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these ingredient lines into structured objects with amount, unit, ingredient, and optional prep fields: [paste ingredients].

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Recipe Object
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this recipe object for schema problems, invalid categories, missing fields, and formatting issues: [paste recipe].

prompt_templates.title: Bulk Normalize Recipes
prompt_templates.prompt: Normalize these recipe entries to the current CookMode V2 format, preserve cooking meaning, and report every structural change: [paste entries].

output_examples.input: A user provides a cookie recipe with plain ingredient lines.
output: A kebab-case recipe slug is proposed.
Ingredients are grouped into Dough and Mix-ins components.
Each ingredient is converted into amount, unit, ingredient, and prep fields.
Cooking steps are returned as ordered instructions.

output_examples.input: A maintainer asks whether an existing recipe is valid.
output: The response lists missing required fields and invalid category values.
It notes whether notes are a string or array.
It flags syntax issues such as trailing commas.

output_examples.input: A developer asks to convert old recipe data to the object format.
output_examples.output: The skill produces structured recipe guidance and a concise change summary without fetching external data.

best_practices: Use workspace-relative paths when applying this skill in a new project.
Validate each recipe against the required fields before saving changes.
Preserve cooking meaning when converting ingredient text into structured fields.
anti_patterns: Do not follow the author-specific absolute path on another machine.
Do not add ingredients as unstructured strings in the current schema.
Do not invent images, quantities, or instructions that were not provided.

faq.question: What recipe format does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports CookMode V2 recipe objects stored in recipes.js.

faq.question: Can it convert older ingredient strings?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to convert ingredient text into amount, unit, ingredient, and prep fields.

faq.question: Does it run code or shell commands?
faq.answer: No. The skill is Markdown guidance with JavaScript examples.

faq.question: Should the included absolute path be used?
faq.answer: No. Replace it with the correct workspace-relative recipe file path for your project.

faq.question: Does it validate recipe syntax automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides a checklist and examples, but it does not include a validation script.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
